content: Sleep disorder treatment is condition-specific. OSA: CPAP (continuous positive airway pressure) is first-line for moderate-severe OSA—eliminates apneas, improves sleepiness, may reduce cardiovascular risk. Weight loss critical (even 10% reduction improves AHI). Alternatives: mandibular advancement devices (mild-moderate OSA), positional therapy (positional OSA), hypoglossal nerve stimulation (Inspire—moderate-severe OSA, CPAP-intolerant, BMI <35), upper airway surgery (uvulopalatopharyngoplasty, maxillomandibular advancement—selected cases). Central sleep apnea: treat underlying cause (optimize heart failure), adaptive servo-ventilation (ASV—contraindicated in HFrEF with LVEF ≤45%), oxygen, acetazolamide. Narcolepsy: stimulants (modafinil/armodafinil first-line, methylphenidate, amphetamines) for sleepiness; sodium oxybate (GHB) for cataplexy and sleepiness (take at night, restricted distribution); pitolisant (histamine H3 inverse agonist); antidepressants (venlafaxine, fluoxetine) for cataplexy. RLS: check ferritin (replete if <75 ng/mL); alpha-2-delta ligands (gabapentin, pregabalin—first-line); dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole—risk of augmentation with long-term use); opioids for refractory cases. Insomnia: CBT-I (cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia—first-line, superior long-term to medications); sleep hygiene; medications short-term only (zolpidem, eszopiclone, ramelteon, doxepin, orexin antagonists like suvorexant).
